html:
XXXIII

Not all those expecting us were in the corridor. Mar√≠a was standing a few rods from the courtyard gate, upon one of the large rocks to our left which commanded the valley. Emma was urging her to come down. We drew near them. Mar√≠a‚Äôs hair, flowing in long and shining curls, showed black against her green muslin dress; she sat down to keep the wind from blowing out her skirts, saying to my sister, who was laughing at her anxiety, ‚ÄúDon‚Äôt you see that I can‚Äôt do it?‚Äù

‚ÄúChild,‚Äù said my father, halfway between alarm and laughter; ‚Äúhow did you ever get up there?‚Äù

She was ashamed of her exploit, and answered, ‚ÄúWe were all alone‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶‚Äù

‚ÄúThat is to say,‚Äù interrupted my father, ‚Äúthat we ought to ride on so that you can come down. How did Emma get down?‚Äù

‚ÄúThe idea!‚ÅÝ‚ÄîI helped her.‚Äù

‚ÄúIt was because I was not afraid,‚Äù said Emma.

‚ÄúCome on, then,‚Äù concluded my father, addressing me; ‚Äúbut have a care.‚Äù

He knew perfectly well that I would stay. Mar√≠a had just said to me with her eyes, ‚ÄúDon‚Äôt go.‚Äù My father got on his horse again, and rode towards the house; I let my horse follow on alone.

‚ÄúHere is where we got up,‚Äù said Mar√≠a, pointing to some scratches and hollows in the rock.

She reached out her rather tremulous hand, and helped me up. I seated myself at her feet, and she said: ‚ÄúDon‚Äôt you see how hard it is? What will papa say! He must think we are crazy.‚Äù

I looked at her without replying. The light in her eyes, timid as they were before mine, and the slight pallor on her cheeks told me, as they so often had, that she was happy.

‚ÄúI shall go on alone,‚Äù said Emma, for the second time, and moved away a few steps to make us believe she would do it.

‚ÄúNo, no, wait a moment,‚Äù said Mar√≠a, rising up.

Seeing that I did not stir, she asked me, ‚ÄúWhat is the matter?‚Äù

‚ÄúWe are very comfortable here.‚Äù

‚ÄúYes, but Emma wants to go, and mamma will be expecting you. Help me get down; I am not afraid now. Let me have your handkerchief.‚Äù

She twisted it up, adding, ‚ÄúYou hold it this way, and when you can‚Äôt reach me with your hand, I will take hold of that.‚Äù

Her plan worked admirably, and once on the ground, she said, ‚ÄúHow will you get down?‚Äù

I leaped from the lowest part of the rock to the turf, and offered her my arm to walk to the house.

‚ÄúIf I had not come, how would you have got down, madcap?‚Äù

‚ÄúI should have got down alone; I was just going to, when you came. But I was afraid of falling, there was so much wind. We climbed up there yesterday, and I got down easily. Why were you so long delayed?‚Äù

‚ÄúWe had to wind up some important matters. What have you been doing all these days?‚Äù

‚ÄúWishing they would pass.‚Äù

‚ÄúIs that all?‚Äù

‚ÄúOh, sewing‚ÅÝ‚Äîand thinking a great deal.‚Äù

‚ÄúWhat about?‚Äù

‚ÄúMany things‚ÅÝ‚Äîwhich one thinks of but does not mention.‚Äù

‚ÄúNot even to me?‚Äù

‚ÄúTo you least of all.‚Äù

‚ÄúHave your own way.‚Äù

‚ÄúBecause you know them already.‚Äù

‚ÄúHaven‚Äôt you read anything?‚Äù

‚ÄúNo, it makes me sad to read alone, and I don‚Äôt like any more those stories ‚ÄòEvenings at the Villa,‚Äô and ‚ÄòAfternoons at the Grange.‚Äô I was going to read some more of Atala, but as you told me there is a passage in it not exactly‚ÅÝ‚ÄîEmma!‚Äù she called, ‚Äúwhy do you hurry on so fast?‚Äù

Emma looked around, smiled, and kept on.

‚ÄúWhat were you doing night before last at ten?‚Äù I asked.

‚ÄúNight before last? Ah, why do you ask me that?‚Äù

‚ÄúI was then very unhappy, thinking of those things which one thinks of but does not mention.‚Äù

‚ÄúNo, no, you can mention them.‚Äù

‚ÄúTell me what you were doing, and then I‚Äôll tell you.‚Äù

‚ÄúI am afraid to.‚Äù

‚ÄúAfraid?‚Äù

‚ÄúPerhaps it is very silly. I was sitting with mamma in the corridor. We heard something that sounded like the shutters of your window, and I thought that they had been left unfastened. I took a light and went to see‚ÅÝ‚Äîwhat nonsense! it makes me afraid to remember what happened.‚Äù

‚ÄúGo on.‚Äù

‚ÄúWe opened the door, and we saw sitting upon one of the shutters which the wind was shaking, a black bird about the size of a very large dove. It uttered a cry such as I had never heard. It seemed to be dazzled by the light I held, and put it out as it flew over our heads just as we were running away in fright. That night I dreamed‚ÅÝ‚Äîbut what makes you look like that?‚Äù

‚ÄúLike what?‚Äù I said, trying to conceal the impression made upon me by her story. What she told me, had taken place at the very hour when my father and I were reading that wretched letter; and that black bird was the same that had brushed my face during the storm, the night that Mar√≠a suffered a return of her attack‚ÅÝ‚Äîthe same that had startled me several times at sunset by sweeping above my head.

‚ÄúLike what?‚Äù replied Mar√≠a. ‚ÄúI see I did wrong to tell you this.‚Äù

‚ÄúCan you imagine that?‚Äù

‚ÄúIt is not imagination.‚Äù

‚ÄúWhat did you dream?‚Äù

‚ÄúI must not tell you.‚Äù

‚ÄúNot even after a while?‚Äù

‚ÄúAlas! never, perhaps.‚Äù

Emma was just opening the door into the courtyard.

‚ÄúWait for us,‚Äù called Mar√≠a; ‚Äúdo, we are truly coming this time.‚Äù

We joined her, and they two walked hand in hand the rest of the way. I felt myself mastered by a vague dread; I was afraid of something, though I could not divine what. But I remembered my father‚Äôs injunction, and endeavored to control myself.
